key_insight: Solid compact stars can deform tidally very differently from fluid stars, producing measurable changes in gravitational-wave signals and strain-driven fracturing during inspiral.
engineering_relevance: This work suggests gravitational-wave and precursor electromagnetic observations could be used to test whether pulsar-like compact stars have solid interiors, informing dense-matter models and compact-object mechanics.
method: Model tidal deformation of strangeon stars with a solid-elastic interior.
Use a shear modulus of μ = 10^34 erg cm^-3 to compare solid vs. fluid tidal deformability.
Simulate strain accumulation during binary inspiral and identify fracture onset and released elastic energy.
evidence_signal: For a 1.4 M⊙ star, the model predicts about 40% difference in tidal deformability between solid and fluid cases, roughly a 10% deviation from the universal I–Love relation, with elastic-energy release up to ~10^46 erg at several hundred Hz.
watch_item: The conclusions depend on the strangeon-star assumption and chosen shear modulus, so applicability to real compact stars remains model-dependent until observations constrain the interior state.
paper_title: Tidal deformation and strain accumulation of solid compact stars
